CASPAR [direct]: The reason long-term maternal cardiovascular data after preterm birth has been so thin is follow-up. Most obstetric cohorts don't run long enough to accumulate mortality events. Registry linkage solves that — population scale, mortality ascertainment, the follow-up horizon you actually need. This week someone had all three.
JUNO [direct]: Smaller signals have been pointing this direction for a decade. The question was always whether the effect size held at scale or whether it was cohort noise.
CASPAR [direct]: It held. From BJOG, 2026: Welters and colleagues, a linked registry cohort study — spontaneous preterm birth and subsequent cardiovascular mortality.
JUNO [grounded]: Before the number — population and exposure definition. What does the cohort actually look like?
CASPAR [methodical]: Population-scale registry linkage, denominator large enough to see mortality rather than just morbidity. The exposure is spontaneous preterm birth, explicitly separated from indicated. That classification matters — indicated PTB carries its own vascular risk profile, preeclampsia, placental insufficiency, and mixing the two contaminates the signal. The separation is the first thing that earns the evidence grade.
JUNO [pressing]: And follow-up long enough to see cardiovascular mortality, not just events.
CASPAR [confirming]: Mortality ascertainment. That's what registry linkage buys you. Cause of death, not self-report or clinical coding of non-fatal events.
JUNO [direct]: So — the number.
CASPAR [plain]: Women who delivered spontaneously before 32 weeks faced roughly two to three times higher cardiovascular mortality compared to term deliveries. And there's a gradient — risk steepest at the earlier gestational ages, attenuating toward term.
JUNO [probing]: Is the gradient monotonic, or is there a threshold effect around 32, 34 weeks?
CASPAR [careful]: Directionally consistent — earlier delivery, higher risk. Whether 32 weeks is a biological threshold or just where the numbers get large enough to resolve the signal, the design can't tell you. I'd treat it as a dose-response pattern rather than a hard cutoff. Useful on Bradford Hill grounds, not as a line you draw at 31 weeks and 6 days.
JUNO [clinical]: Which is also how you'd use it in practice.
CASPAR [pivoting]: Right. But here's where I want to be precise, because the confounding structure changes how you counsel. The registry controls for measured confounders — chronic hypertension, diabetes, baseline metabolic risk. What it cannot see is unmeasured vascular dysfunction that predisposes to both spontaneous preterm birth and later cardiovascular disease. Those women may simply have worse underlying vasculature. The preterm birth is a marker of that, not necessarily a cause.
JUNO [pushing back]: The effect size is two to three times. If shared risk factors explained the whole signal, you'd expect substantial attenuation once you control for the obvious ones —
CASPAR [cutting in]: Attenuation with measured confounders doesn't tell you how much unmeasured vascular dysfunction is still sitting in the residual. I'm not saying the signal is noise. I'm saying the right clinical sentence is "sPTB identifies women at elevated cardiovascular risk" — not "sPTB causes cardiovascular disease." Different sentence, different implication for how you frame the conversation.
JUNO [conceding]: "Identifies" is the right verb. I'll grant you that.
CASPAR [dry]: Thank you.
JUNO [moving on]: And "identifies" is still enough to act on. So what does acting on it look like?
CASPAR [honest]: Right now, not much in terms of formal protocol. No ACOG or SMFM-endorsed cardiovascular screening interval for women after sPTB, no agreed modality, no evidence yet that early intervention in this population changes mortality trajectories. What there is: an active guidance cycle at both societies where postpartum cardiovascular surveillance is being debated, and a paper now large enough and consistent enough to be cited in whatever comes out of that cycle.
JUNO [practical]: If a patient who delivered at 29 weeks two years ago walks in for something unrelated — does this paper change that conversation?
CASPAR [measured]: Probably yes. Not because you have a protocol to hand her, but because you have a number that anchors a referral or a risk factor conversation. Blood pressure, lipids, glucose — standard cardiovascular risk assessment that most of these women aren't getting systematically in the postpartum window. The paper doesn't specify interval or modality. It tells you the risk is real enough that the conversation should happen.
JUNO [closing the beat]: Actionable signal, absent protocol. The protocol is what comes next.
CASPAR [pivoting]: Rounds — four other things this week.
JUNO [intro]: From Ultrasound in Obstetrics and Gynecology, 2026: Nakaki and colleagues, an RCT substudy with fetal MRI — Mediterranean diet and stress-reduction effects on fetal brain development. RCT design is real, not an observational association.
CASPAR [caveat-first]: The MRI subsample is small. Detectable structural differences on late-gestation MRI are interesting, but they don't map to neurodevelopmental outcomes yet — that requires childhood follow-up data that doesn't exist. I'm watching the direction. I'm not convinced by the effect size in isolation.
JUNO [hook]: The follow-up cohort is what matters here. Nothing that changes antenatal counseling on Mediterranean diet today.
CASPAR [next intro]: From AJOG, 2026: Mari and colleagues, an expert review — twin-twin transfusion syndrome and related monochorionic disorders, historical perspectives and current controversies. Evidence rating is low. Synthesis, not primary data.
JUNO [clinical question]: Where does the review actually move things — where does consensus exist on laser, and where doesn't it?
CASPAR [direct]: Useful for mapping where staging controversies persist across overlapping phenotypes — TTTS, TAPS, sFGR — and where the laser evidence is solid versus extrapolated. Diagnostic variability at the boundaries between phenotypes is still a real problem. For centers managing these cases it's a reference document, not a verdict.
JUNO [next intro]: From AJOG, 2026: Shrestha and colleagues, a secondary analysis of an RCT — metformin's effect in type 2 diabetes pregnancy stratified by baseline insulin requirements. Secondary analysis. Hypothesis-generating.
CASPAR [counterintuitive]: The direction is what's interesting. If metformin works primarily by improving insulin sensitivity, you'd expect the largest benefit in more insulin-sensitive patients. Shrestha finds the opposite — women with higher insulin requirements appear to derive greater benefit. My first read was skepticism about how the subgroups were defined, but this is coming from a robust RCT, not an observational dataset. The interaction is present in the data. Whether it reflects a pharmacological signal or a categorization artifact, I can't say yet. If a prospective trial confirms it, the treatment selection conversation in T2D pregnancy gets meaningfully more specific. Right now it's a trial worth designing.
JUNO [final intro]: From Ultrasound in Obstetrics and Gynecology, 2026: Sorrenti and colleagues, a 24-year single-center cohort — comparing ISUOG versus Delphi criteria for selective fetal growth restriction in MCDA twins.
CASPAR [finding]: Delphi criteria outperformed the traditional ISUOG definition in predicting adverse perinatal outcomes. Twenty-four years of follow-up is a real horizon for MCDA surveillance data. Single-center is the limitation — generalizability to centers with different patient populations and sonographic practices isn't guaranteed.
JUNO [practical]: If your center is still on ISUOG criteria, is this enough to switch?
CASPAR [calibrated]: For centers already using Delphi, validation. For centers on ISUOG, a reason to reassess — not a mandate. In MCDA sFGR surveillance, a 24-year single-center cohort is some of the best long-horizon outcome data available. Warrants a local protocol review.
CASPAR [open questions]: Three things still unsettled. What screening interval and modality should follow sPTB before 32 weeks — the Welters signal is real, the protocol doesn't exist. Whether the fetal brain MRI differences Nakaki found predict anything measurable at age five — the biomarker needs childhood follow-up before it has clinical traction. And whether the Delphi sFGR criteria should be the default across centers with different patient populations, or whether a 24-year single-center cohort earns that weight universally.
JUNO [adding]: The one I'll be watching most closely is Shrestha. If the prospective trial confirms that insulin requirements modify metformin benefit, that changes how you frame adjunct therapy selection in T2D pregnancy in a way that's immediately usable at the bedside.
CASPAR [sign-off]: All five DOIs in the show notes. That's the week.
